baseline_dominance.justification: The construction hides the full bitmask from the RS (predicate-hiding), which RFC 7662 per-RS introspection policy cannot do if the predicate is chosen at presentation time. However, rfc7662_advocate Attack 1 correctly identifies that the construction does not clearly exhibit a runtime-adaptive predicate — the bitmask is fixed at enrollment and the circuit checks a static mask comparison. If the required mask is known at issuance, the AS can mint a JWT encoding exactly the needed subset. WIMSE/SPIFFE cover AS-blind presentation. The genuine gap (predicate-hiding from RS) exists but is not sharply articulated as the primary claim, and the baseline can approximate with per-RS scoped tokens for the static case.

formal_security_argument.justification: A threat model exists (adversarial AS, on-chain observers), an SP game is described, and a reduction sketch covers credential commitment hiding via assumption A5. However: (a) cryptographer Attack 1 — no simulator is exhibited, public inputs/outputs of the circuit are never enumerated, and HVZK vs SE-ZK is not stated; (b) cryptographer Attack 2 — Groth16 trusted setup combined with an adversarial-AS threat model is a fundamental category error (adversarial AS that participated in the ceremony can forge proofs via toxic waste); (c) cryptographer Attack 3 — nullifier precomputation under predictable scope_id is unaddressed. The reduction sketch exists but critical assumptions are vague or contradictory.

implementability.justification: Construction maps directly to existing Bolyra primitives: Poseidon2 and Poseidon5 (existing arities), Num2Bits(128) (standard Circom gadget), BabyJubjub EdDSA (existing), Groth16 over BN254 (existing). Circuit cost is explicitly stated at ~11,330 constraints, well within the pot16.ptau (2^16) budget. The change is surgical — one new private input, one Poseidon2 wrap, one range check. Phase 1 compatible.

adversarial_survival.justification: Multiple Tier 3 attacks found issues the construction cannot answer: (1) cryptographer Attack 2 — Groth16 + adversarial-AS is a category error; toxic waste holder can forge any proof, destroying the core differentiator; (2) cryptographer Attack 4 — cumulative encoding invariant is NOT enforced in AgentPolicy circuit, only in Delegation, allowing a malicious operator to prove predicates over structurally invalid bitmasks; (3) spiffe_engineer Attack 1 — the operator key IS the AS, making the adversarial-AS claim circular; (4) rfc7662_advocate Attack 1 — the runtime-adaptive predicate that would separate this from per-RS JWT scoping is never exhibited in the circuit. These are not mitigated weaknesses — they are unanswered structural flaws.

scenario_fit.justification: Two scenarios are named ('regulated agent with 2^64 permission space', 'AS is semi-trusted') but both are hypothetical. No concrete deployment with a named stakeholder or specific regulatory requirement is identified. The actual bitmask is 8 bits (not 2^64). The CU scenario is actively undermined by cu_ciso attacks (FIPS non-compliance, audit trail destruction, missing key custody spec). No named credit union, fintech, or agent platform is cited as a concrete adopter where this property is load-bearing.

gaps: Groth16 + adversarial-AS is a category error: either switch to PLONK/transparent SRS for this circuit or narrow the threat model to passive-AS only
Exhibit the runtime-adaptive predicate that the RS cannot know at issuance time — without this, per-RS JWT scoping matches the construction
Add cumulative-encoding constraints (bitmask[3]*(1-bitmask[2])===0 etc.) to AgentPolicy circuit, not just Delegation
Enumerate all public inputs/outputs of the circuit and state whether ZK is HVZK or SE-ZK; exhibit or cite the simulator
Address nullifier precomputation: if AS knows scope_ids and observes enrollments, deterministic nullifiers enable linkability
Clarify that adversarial-AS means honest-but-curious (learns RS-presented scope) not actively malicious (issues false credentials) — the operator key IS the issuing authority
Name a concrete deployment with a specific stakeholder (e.g., a named fintech platform, agent framework, or CU core provider) where predicate-hiding is load-bearing
Add FIPS bridge argument or explicitly scope out regulated financial deployments where Poseidon/BN254 are non-compliant
Add auditor-escrow architecture for the hidden bitmask to satisfy NCUA/FFIEC audit trail requirements if CU deployment is in scope
Specify key custody requirements for credentialBlinding (HSM, rotation semantics, blast radius on compromise)
